part.text:
## Verdict
**DISAGREE** — confidence 0.85 — the proposal has a fatal dimensional contradiction (480×480 in research contract vs 560×480 in spec/harness) that would produce invalid tiles, plus the repair pass assumes GLM can self-correct the same spatial reasoning it demonstrably lacks.

---

## What GPT-5.5 got right
- Correctly identifies that prose contracts are unenforceable under instruction load and that a **deterministic post-render gate** is the right authority model (gate owns acceptance, model never self-certifies).
- The six gate checks (title-band intrusion, visual-panel overflow, bbox overlap, banned-gray-on-dark, `text-transform:uppercase`, clipping) are well-targeted at RC-1..RC-5.
- The arm-isolation design (C0 / T1 / T2) enables clean A/B inference.
- Recognizing that preflight self-reports are untrustworthy and the gate must measure the rendered DOM is the correct posture.

---

## Gaps / risks / errors

### 1. FATAL: Dimensional contradiction between contract and harness
The A1 research recommendation hardcodes **480×480** geometry (`--tile-w:480px; --tile-h:480px;`, visual box `y=24..328`). The spec *correctly* overrides this to **560×480** (matching the existing harness). But the plan, architecture section, and A1 recommendation block in the spec itself all copy-paste the **480×480** values without reconciling them. A GLM prompt that says "the tile is 560×480" followed by CSS vars that declare `--tile-w:480px; --tile-h:480px; --visual-bottom:328px; --title-y:352px` will produce tiles positioned for a 480×480 canvas rendered in a 560×480 container — guaranteed layout breakage on every tile. This must be resolved before any code is written.

### 2. The repair pass is structurally unsound
REQ-007 sends "failure JSON + rendered screenshot" to the same GLM-5.2 that produced the failing tile. RC-1 and RC-2 are *spatial reasoning defects* — GLM cannot compute cumulative row heights or detect bounding-box collisions. Giving it JSON that says "element X overflowed by 42px" does not give it the constraint-solving capability to fix the overflow. The model will either (a) guess at new pixel values (likely producing a different but still-wrong layout), or (b) strip content to fit (silently dropping rows, shrinking text). The edge case "fails twice: no second repair, keep best-scoring pass" confirms this — the repair is expected to have a high failure rate. A repair pass on a model that *can't do the thing* is token burn, not remediation.

**Alternative**: Skip the GLM repair pass entirely in phase 001. Instead, mark gate-failing tiles and defer actual fixes to phase 004 (skeleton-first 2D, where layout coordinates are computed deterministically). This eliminates ~20-30% of the token budget (~800-1400 tokens per failed tile × expected ~18 failures = 14k-25k wasted tokens).

### 3. The "as-text vs as-fill" contrast distinction is under-specified and likely harder than estimated
The contrast gate must not flag `#8591b3` when used as an SVG `stroke=` (aangepast-assortiment-4 scored 88) but must flag it as readable text `color:` on dark. This requires traversing the DOM, distinguishing text-bearing nodes from decorative ones, and checking *which property* carries a given hex value. For SVG, `stroke=` vs `fill=` is parseable; for HTML/CSS, you need to check computed `color` vs `border-color` vs `background-color` for each element. The spec says "dedicated `contrast.mjs`" but provides no algorithm. The effort estimate (3-5 hours for gate + contrast combined) is optimistic by at least 2× for getting this right, especially with SVG inline in GLM output.

### 4. Causal inference gap in the arm design
T1 = contract only, T2 = contract + gate + repair. There is no **gate-only (no contract)** arm. If T2 outperforms T1, you can't attribute the lift to the gate + repair vs the contract. If T2 is *worse* than T1 (contract alone is sufficient), you can't detect that the gate/repair is harmful. Add a T3 = gate-only arm (gate the control output, no contract, no repair) to isolate each component's contribution.

### 5. Token budget math doesn't close
NFR-P01 claims "~350-450 prompt tokens" by replacing diffuse layout prose. But the A1 contract block alone is ~430 tokens (I counted: CSS vars paragraph + layout primitive paragraph + caps paragraph + text/case paragraph + dark tokens paragraph + 6-gate checklist), and the A2 reasoning-embedded contract adds another ~500 tokens. The preflight JSON schema adds ~200 tokens. Total is ~1100-1200 tokens of new contract content. Unless the old layout prose was exactly that long (unlikely for "diffuse" prose), this is a net addition. The IFScale cliff risk (mentioned in spec §6) is real: more instruction density → more dropped constraints → paradoxical regression on the linear-flow tiles the proposal is trying to protect. SC-005's "do not regress >3 pts" guardrail may not hold.

### 6. Missing gate failure modes
- No handling for GLM output that crashes the headless renderer (JS infinite loop, `document.write` in a loop, enormous inline SVG). The spec says "render or browser failure surfaces as an explicit gate error" but doesn't define recovery.
- No handling for malformed HTML where elements exist but CSS is unparseable. Does every element without a style attribute get a pass? Fail?
- No handling for the gate itself being wrong (false positive sends a good tile to unnecessary repair; false negative ships a bad tile). There's no gate self-audit mechanism.

### 7. SC-001 (76-82% SHIP) is over-optimistic
The 60% → 76-82% prediction assumes RC-1..RC-5 account for all 18 failures (27/45), and that the contract + gate + repair fixes all of them. But RC-1 alone has 8 tiles with overflow (only some of which are the 18 failures), and RC-2 has collision issues on tiles that may *also* have other defects not in RC-1..RC-5. The proposal doesn't map each of the 18 failures to a specific RC with a predicted fix probability. A more honest forecast: contract alone fixes ~5-7 tiles (overflow + title-band + text-transform are cheap to fix in prompt only on the tiles where GLM was *close* to correct), gate catches ~10-12 failures, repair fixes ~0-2 of those. Net: 32-36/45 = 71-80%, with high variance. The upper bound is plausible; the lower bound should be acknowledged.

---

## Strongest improvement or alternative

**Replace the GLM repair pass with a deterministic CSS fixup pass.**

Instead of asking GLM (which can't do spatial reasoning) to fix its own overflow, write a 50-line post-processor that:
1. Reads the gate failure JSON
2. For overflow: clamps the visual panel to `max-height: calc(var(--visual-h))` with `overflow: hidden` and shrinks font-size by 10% iteratively until all children fit (purely algorithmic, needs zero model tokens)
3. For title-band intrusion: sets `z-index` on title elements above visual panel, adds a solid background to the title band
4. For `text-transform: uppercase`: regex-replaces it with `text-transform: none` in the CSS
5. For banned gray tokens: regex-replaces the banned hexes with the approved `#E7ECF7`/`#B8C2D6` tokens on text-bearing elements only

This achieves 80% of the repair intent with zero model calls, zero token cost, and deterministic behavior. It's also a natural stepping stone: if the fixup pass succeeds on 70% of failures, phase 004's skeleton-first approach handles the remaining 30%. The GLM repair path becomes unnecessary.

---

## One thing to test or verify before building this phase

**Verify that the headless browser bbox-overlap check can correctly classify the 5 known-bad and 5 known-good tiles as of today.**

Before writing any contract code, take the *existing* control-arm output for accountbeheer-4 (35), orders-facturen-4 (52), goedkeuringssysteem-4 (55), oci-4 (58), aangepast-assortiment-3 (58) and the 5 scoring-90+ tiles. Run them through a prototype gate (even a manual one with Playwright + `page.evaluate(() => document.querySelectorAll('*').map(el => el.getBoundingClientRect()))`). Confirm:
1. The gate's bbox overlap check flags at least 4/5 known-bad tiles as failing
2. The gate's bbox overlap check passes at least 4/5 known-good tiles (no false positives)
3. The contrast as-text/as-fill logic correctly passes `#8591b3` as SVG stroke in aangepast-assortiment-4

If (1) fails, the gate can't catch what you need it to catch. If (2) fails at >20% false positive rate, the gate will waste repair calls on good tiles. If the as-text/as-fill logic can't be made correct on the first real tile tested, the 3-5 hour estimate for the full contrast module is wrong and should be revised upward before committing to the phase schedule. This is a 1-hour smoke test that could save 8-15 hours of building the wrong gate.
